100 passengers on the Celebrity Infinity cruise ship have fallen ill with a gastrointestinal disease, with symptoms including vomiting and diarrhea. The CDC is investigating the cause. This is the third time the ship has experienced such an outbreak, with previous incidents in 2006 and 2013.

Gastrointestinal illness has affected 100 people on the Celebrity Infinity cruise ship, including 95 passengers and 5 crew members. The CDC is investigating the cause of the illness. This is the third outbreak on the ship, which has taken measures to address the situation.

A gastrointestinal illness has affected about 100 people on the Celebrity Infinity cruise ship, including both passengers and crew members. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) has yet to determine the cause of the illness, but two CDC staffers will meet the ship in San Diego on Monday to investigate further. This is the third time the Celebrity Infinity has experienced such an outbreak, which has led to increased cleaning and disinfection procedures and specimen collection for testing.

text: Gastrointestinal illness has affected 100 people on the cruise ship Celebrity Infinity, according to the CDC. Symptoms include vomiting and diarrhea. The cause is unknown and two CDC staffers will meet the ship in San Diego on Monday. This is the third outbreak on the ship, which has a history of sanitation issues.

Almost 2,000 inscriptions from World War I soldiers have been found in an underground city, located beneath battlefields near Naours, France. The graffiti dates back to almost 100 years and includes names, addresses, and military details of soldiers from Australia, the US, and the UK. Photographer Jeff Gusky, who has been documenting the site, described the inscriptions and the underground city as "breathtaking". The underground city dates back centuries but was sealed up in the 18th century, and rediscovered in the late 19th century. During WWI, soldiers would take refuge in the carved-out rooms and pathways. Gusky has noted 1,821 names, about 40% of which are Australian, with most of the others being British.

text: A collection of graffiti dating back almost 100 years has been discovered in a French chalk quarry near Naours, France. The inscriptions were made by World War I soldiers, including Australians, Americans, and British soldiers. The underground city, where the graffiti was found, was used as a refuge by soldiers during the war. The discovery of the graffiti and the extensive underground city has only recently come to light due to a change in ownership of the land. The photographer, Jeff Gusky, who has documented the site, describes it as "breathtaking" and a "treasure trove." The graffiti provides a human connection to the soldiers who lived a century ago.

entities.text: A collection of graffiti dating back almost 100 years
